speaker: interviewer
text: Mary Stolar. OK. Can you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We were wearing this white arm bands, we started out looking-- going working for the Germans-- not only because of the feudal-- we weren't paid anyway, but maybe-- they were giving us bread. Bread was the main thing, but also perhaps we were telling-- it was told that if you are employed for Germans we will avoid other trouble-- trouble with-- of course, obviously them, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody in there, they called us [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] which means a statement that you are employed in-- in our unit or something, you know? Even-- even cleaning for them and doing things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now after-- well I find a place where I cleaned and I washed for soldiers, they were stationed in Rovno, where they've got some individual places where they slept, and they were fairly nice to me. I was young and-- and kind of attractive. And I learned the language, picked up every word, every word-- I'm very good at languages-- that I picked up. And I was-- in daylight they took me with my son, Lieber-- we called him Lieber-- later we changed it, by the way, to Zbyszek.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- how? What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zbyshek Z-b-- Z-b-y-s-h-e-k.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started to go on with those things. After a little while, I can't remember, it was very short time, the posters and the rumors, we are going to have to wear yellow [NON-ENGLISH]. They call this in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. In German they call this, [GERMAN], I can't-- [GERMAN] isn't German. But-- they called this [GERMAN], or-- a [GERMAN] means a stain. [GERMAN]. And we were removed, they forget about the white arm bands-- was too good to be nice. And we're going to wear two of them. One will go here, on the left side where your heart, and the other one will go in the middle of the back. You have to sew it-- they told you even how to sew it up. Got to- you will be penalized when you go out and walk around without it. We started to where this [GERMAN]. The rest of the time, terrible this-- oh-- God, when I think about it. When I think about. The world didn't do nothing about it. Anyway-- it was-- you know, when I walked with this [GERMAN], it was also my impression, somebody-- one of the teenagers, the anti-Semites, they're going to kick me beside, you know, because they know that I identified as a Jew. And it was a discrimination. It was a very, very low, low-- low-- without any dignity, without any human respect. Everywhere, that now, after we started to wear this [GERMAN], or before, I can't give you the exact dates. This is something I never wrote it down and I never--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. He's just going to mention to the neighbors that we're video taping upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well-- I'll tell-- I'll telephone them.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. We keep going here. We keep going. It's OK. Continue on with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Those neighbors upstairs, they are not nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're vacuuming. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should've--

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry. No problem. You can just continue with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, I should have told them. They would maybe listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue please, Mrs. Stolar.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't even open the door to animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just continue with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they were starting out moving into the ghetto. Start-- that was the third thing-- there was arm bands. Well, the thing is, also I missed it, the minute-- the minute they marched in, they collected a few men. We were hiding because of the-- of the bombs. They collected a few men, and we all heard the news, the men never came back. A German soldiers collected some men for work, whatever, they never came back. Started the hell, already. We moved into the ghetto, they-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was-- we told the ghetto is completely closed. I told her that-- I-- I told her that I'm going to have people coming Thursday morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't go-- continue on with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to hear what did they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, no let's talk about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'm sorry. We started to move into those apartments. They were emptied out of Gentiles, and they are prepared for us-- kind of. It was several streets--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say Rob? They were OK? OK. Continue on-- please continue on with the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. They-- it was open the ghetto-- it was open. I told you, the ghetto is closed completely, you can't walk out, it's going to be. But somebody said, the Warsaw ghetto is different because they were-- it's a different kind of style. First of all, there was a different plan to begin-- they're going to be killed, the ghetto, the Warsaw ghetto, the Jews. This one was-- soon, soon, soon, soon get rid of it and there was no geographically to cover, to close it. Not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was streets and they call us Judenviertel. Judenviertel is called-- Juden are Jews, viertel is quarters. Jewish quarters, in Polish they call dzielnica, which is in Polish the same translation. In Yiddish they called this in the ghetto, in ghetto. We named it ghetto in Yiddish, and in German Judenviertel. And you were only exclusively living in this particular, several streets. There weren't too many, I mean. Oh, wait a minute. I'm sorry, I missed something-- a very important factor. I go, I have to go back for a little while. After we started to wear those-- those-- those armbands, or the [YIDDISH], I don't remember which one was it. There was new, new news posted-- all the Jews from Rovno have to go, have to report to the Sosenki. Sosenki was called a-- alas a wood. Wood-- how do you say what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know where trees and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Woods. Woods. There is another term also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forest-- oh. There was in this particular forest called, in Polish, Sosenki. This comes from trees, the name, Sosenki-- what is very famous-- you have to write it down-- called Sosenki. All the Jews are going to have to be reported tomorrow morning. All in this particular place called Sosenki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knows what they're going to be done with them. And I'll never forget when we started to march down. Oh, God, please. [CRYING] My son was having-- was recently recovering from measles. And it was an awful, I don't remember the name. There is books written about Rovno-- it's properly all the dates. And I don't need it, I know everything by myself, because this is where the people who never been there. They went to Russia and ran away. Talking-- oh, I have to go back also a little while.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war in 1941, the-- the Germans occupied our part of the-- of the Poland. Jewish people, you know the ones who recover, they all run to Russia-- automatically. My son keep saying, mama why didn't you, why didn't you run together? But I was following my husband's family. I didn't have my own already, they were all  spread--

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing-- oh, God, help me. They killed my father, they-- they marched in, they killed-- not Germans, Ukrainians, they took out my father from his home and killed him. He was-- my mother, my stepmother died before the war of tuberculosis. She died in hospital of tuberculosis. Tuberculosis for the poor, especially the poor, was very popular at the time. My father was having a nice lady friend, whatever, and he was living with her. They went into-- she came running to me. They went into the room, they took him out middle of the night, she never saw. A very nice woman, a Rovno woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was such a short relationship I didn't get involved with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going with my son, holding him, going to the-- to the-- to the headquarters of the-- of the government. There was a nice German, he was a major, with the uniform. I went, I started to speak a little German, and I was going there and begging to find out what happened to my father. Let him out-- he's just a poor innocent man, he was never involved in politics. And this nice major, with the uniform, elderly man, he remembers the First World War. And he says frau-- [GERMAN] dear lady. I can't help it, I'm going to try. Nothing happened. He was very upset about it when I told him. But other things-- he could see by himself what's going on. He was one of those decent persons. You know, later when I went once more, I thought perhaps he can help with some food. They said he wasn't there anymore. He just resigned, he went to his country, which was Austria. I was told he came from Austria. My father, I never saw him. OK. Then it started out with the Sosenki. The Sosenki.

speaker: interviewer
text: We started out, all of the neighbors asked, are you going? Are you going tomorrow morning? And saying, of course we have to. They are going maybe place us to work. They wouldn't kill us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other say, no I'm going to hide, I'm going to this. And my son was recovering from measles. And I got only my husband's family. There was my family, was just broken up. I didn't know what happened. My mother was automatically probably, in [NON-ENGLISH] in which she's staying with the other siblings. They was definitely killed, all of them. And we didn't have any contact. I'm talking about myself, my father, my son, my husband. My husband, again, my husband, Chiam, my son's father, was mobilized, drafted, when the Russians were-- were there. Still not the Germans, shortly before. He was drafted, and I went with my son to say good bye to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was wearing the helmet, and I was holding my-- holding it, two-years-old-- two and a half-- three-year-old son. He was crazy about him. Saying-- saying good bye, and saying, Lieber, make [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] means salute. And he taught him how to make salute. And he goes down and he makes salute to his father. And his father is so impressed. You know, like parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my-- the the last time I saw my husband and my son, last time he saw his father. It was in the '41, when the war started with the Germans, that's it. We're going back to Sosenki. Then I went-- we started to walk. And then I got my husband's family only left. My mother-in-law, my husband's sister with her husband, another sister-- well, anyway. I started to walk with my son. It was a very wet snow, a miserable wet and like the situation like the humor of the Jews. And people walking, thousands of them, everybody. And it was even the sick ones, was-- the Germans-- the young, the Hitler Youth-- they were young people serving the army. Hitler Youth, I don't know if you heard youth, Hitler Youth. They were wearing red swastikas, they were identified by their uniform of the red swastikas. This Hitler Youth was holding, there were carriages and-- horses and carriages. The ones, the elderly, they couldn't walk, they took them out with us. Even from the hospital, even from the orphanage, the children. They all-- are huge-- I'll never-- this is like happened yesterday-- a huge crowd. We were all walking. And I was holding my son, and I was terrified because of the measles can-- you can catch cold, can be complications. Then I decided my way to the Sosenki, I'm going to go to stop into my sister-in-law and see what they are planning. You see, I didn't have anybody except them. I stopped in and she said, my sister and my brothers-- no, no you don't go any place. We are there already. My other sister-in-law's children, and my sister, and the whole family was already there. My husband's family, I'm talking about, and so was I with my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I avoided the Sosenki, otherwise I would be killed right way. Then we all find out, what did they do in Sosenki with these people? What was it? What was fate of it? What was the fate of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we knew already graves were prepared. Graves-- now they have this thing every year, the people from Rovno, left of here. I don't even going, I know. Those are people who never-- they run. You see, I am the only, perhaps the two or three survivor from Rovno, who is still-- One is in Israel. Musha is in Israel, she survived. But in different circumstances, all together. My-- my tragedy was the child, because he was circumcised-- naturally he was circumcised. This was the hell of my hiding. There was nobody circumcised-- not any Pole, nobody circumcised except the Jews. And when they pull down his panties, and that is going to be the end of it. Then this Musha who is in Israel, she was by herself, and she was very famous, very rich girl. And she got lots of dollars to save herself. And she got a different kind of time to survive. Not a quarter what I was through. We talk about it with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so continue on with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when I stayed with my family and then the Germans were going, they were-- we were told OK, they were killed over there. They said, prisoners-- Russian prisoners of war, because the war started with the Russians, as you know, people. The war-- in '41, '39 now, no, was a pact. '41 was the start of the war of the Russians with the Germans, as you know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they have got a lots, the beginning, they were very strong, Germans were very prepared for war. Boy, they were very lucky, and they were going all the way to Leningrad, to Moscow. And they got a lot of-- how do you call it the W's? Prisoners of war, POW? They've got a lot of prisoners of war, and they said the Russian prisoners were digging the graves that was prepared for the Jews in Sosenki.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know nothing of what happened. We find out later. One say this, and we got still a little hope-- maybe they put him into a camp, and they were working. They will give them-- use of them. They will have use of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, we-- I was glad I avoided this. We don't know for how long. And then we went, pushed into this ghetto-- into this Dzielnica, into this Judenviertel. It was a small part that left. I don't know how many-- thousands? Must be very small part after the Sosenki, the bulk of the Jews were already killed, this was the bulk, this is in the history. And I've read a lot of them run away to Russia, and the ones who were remaining was in the dzielnica, in the Judenviertel.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on with our lives, we were-- we were going on with our lives. I started to work for the Germans, I worked already-- I cleaned. We were doing-- we were even joking with the soldiers. Many of them were very anti-Nazis. Many of them were asking, and they all talked to-- when they wouldn't see the [YIDDISH], they would think we are-- I-- I wasn't Jewish. But they knew it. They saw it, and they didn't say anything. And we were giving bread, we were giving margarine, we are giving-- this was a big help for me. And my mother-in-law survived-- my mother-in-law, my two sister-in-laws and my brother-in-law, the other one-- and their two children. One is in Israel, the girl, one is in Israel. The other one was killed. Now, to hear them later was killed-- it's the other sister. It's so confusing. Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I moved into ghetto with my mother-in-law, and she took care of the child when I went to the Germans to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I remember I hated so much the [YIDDISH] to wear it, and she will say, you removed it, you removed it. You are going to be sent to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going, why everybody's wearing it, well why do you care? Well, anyway, we are having argument. Now, I was going to-- once and we were very short, in food short. We were without food almost. We were exchanging with the peasants a piece of material is very important, clothes, or whatever you have. The Rovno peasants which is more Ukrainians, the peasants were most of them Ukrainians, we were exchanging, and we will get some flour, a little bit of potatoes. Then they were giving out bread in a bakery, and there was a long-- long line, a long line only for non-Jews. Only for Poles, non-Jews. I took a chance, I wore a kerchief. The kerchief, you know the point of the-- the-- the-- the front [NON-ENGLISH], the front part, which is patch, right? What-- don't you-- patchwork? Patchwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: You call it, what is it you call it? A patch?

speaker: interviewer
text: Patch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, patch. I will cover my son, I hold him kind-- kind of innocently. You don't see it because I'm holding my son. It's not my fault, I'm not trying to hide it. The back is the [NON-ENGLISH], the babushka, the kerchief, will cover the back, the [NON-ENGLISH] in the back. I went into the line, standing in the bakery. I remember, I did that, I accomplished something. I received a loaf of bread, which was a jewel-- a jewel. But once I remember, I'll never forget this, this in my mind, I was standing there, in the back, in the line, and one bitch of a girl, a Polish girl who recognize me-- she went over to the Germans, she didn't know they are too stupid to learn another language. She took him for his sleeve and, mister, mister,

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh you have to sit down, you have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. I'm sorry John.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mister, mister. This for his sleeve. Jude, Jude. The only word she learned is Jude. Jude was a male, and Juden, Juden is a female. But anyway, mister, Jude, Jude, and pointed at me. And I said to myself, oh, my God, oh, my God. He pulled me out of the line, and he started screaming at me. And my son, he was just a baby. He started, mama, mama, cover me. He was scared. And he didn't do me-- he didn't-- he didn't punch me, he didn't take me to a concentration camp. Disappear, I don't want to see you. And then I figure, uh-oh, I was lucky to avoid this hell. And I went, I came home and I told my mother-in-law. And she say, of course, I told you. One of those days you be in trouble, you're stubborn. This is one incident. OK. We're going now in the ghetto, going to work, and everybody was-- very few-- there were quite a few were working for them. It was a [NON-ENGLISH] a factory they opened to-- to sew uniforms, and-- and fix uniforms and sew uniforms. There were very quite a few Jews employed there. And we'd say to ourselves, no they need us, there is so little left, they need us. And we were hoping, living with hope, and going on with our lives. Jewish people, they take programs in the history of Jews, and they always survive. You know how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: After a little while and, I don't know again the dates, they said, did you hear? They are going to finish out the ghetto. Did you? Again the same story, and I didn't believe in it, they didn't believe it. Meantime, then I saw, I'll never forget, I saw a German highly officer riding a horse, moving around in the-- in the streets in the ghetto. And I was such a-- I was wise guy, coming over and I said to him, sir. Back then, because the rumors had started, no, no, no they said only the children, they'll take away the children, they'll take away the children. I said, sir, is going to be something that will take the children? Will remove the children from the parents? And I'll never forget he was smiling, oh no little frau, dear lady. Oh no, don't worry, oh no. And I told that later and she said, oh Mary, in heaven's name, you believe what he said? Well, well, well, they started out in the evening to-- go claiming the things where the electricity-- what do you call? Outside, the things where the electricities are connected? The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, the-- The poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: The poles-- started climbing the poles, the soldiers, the German soldiers, and fixing something there. They didn't like it, because there was no electricity in the ghetto. And we said, something is not like this, or kosher. And we heard the old stories-- somebody came in and said, yes they are taking us during the night. They will cleaning-- cleaning-- going to clean us out. And everybody who has a some of their friend, a Gentile friend. You have somebody you know, somebody you used to go to school, somebody, some-- and the truth of the matter is, they were punished. The Polack who's going-- going to hide you was punished almost same way-- deadly. Which in way, in a way I explain it, I don't blame them, but whatever opportunities they could, and they would, and they even helped out. Now I've got a friend, his name was Kola, which comes from Nikolai. Kola, K-O-L-A. And I know him, he was a Polack. And he was having-- when he was a-- he was a very handsome man, and he was also active in playing football. In football they got accident from playing football, and he lost a leg. It was at a time they were so primitive, no antibiotics, nothing. And they were operating him several times in his leg, he was still in his teens, and he lost his leg. Anyway, he was still looking-- going with a cane and he looked very attractive, and he was kind of intelligent. He was-- we used to know each other, like it's a small town after all, compared to America. He would say, hi. I never was dating him, I never was-- He would say-- I would walk by, hi Mary, Mara, and something. And he knew my husband, also, from just, hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he came in the ghetto, once before the rumors, brought me some bread, and became a little acquainted with him. When it started those rumors, and people say, you have somebody you know, a Gentile? You have someone, a Gentile? The best thing is go and hide. Get out with the night, and that they will see, maybe it will come out nothing out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said he's-- I should go-- he's going to take me with him. I took my child, I didn't have much to wear, it was in a hot day. Maybe in July, maybe August, and this I can remember, but I don't know the date. I took my child, he was hardly wearing a little-- a little overall without, and I was having only a-- excuse my expression-- panties and a bra, nothing with me. I didn't take anything, I didn't have anything to take too much anyway, but perhaps another dress eventually. We ran to-- we went in the evening. He was living with his sister and his brother-in-law. Kola was living with his sister and his brother-in-law, with a little boy who was my son's age. When we came there, you can imagine how I could spend the night. They weren't crazy about seeing me, for two reasons-- because I'm-- they're anti-Semites, obviously, automatically. And secondly, it was a danger to hide the Jews, you know. He did it for his feelings for me, was a different picture-- not for his being so sentimental or being so loyal or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now over the night it was-- well I'll never this night-- forget the night. In the morning, the woman came into the living in the middle because in East European countries you deliver the-- deliver the milk every morning in a big-- in a big aluminum can but, well, whatever. And she said, did you hear? Did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you hear, they pinched out the Jews-- they two of them lying when even-- when I walked, she said, I saw two of them lying. They said they were running and they shot after them. They came in with trucks and they picked, pulled them, put them into the trucks and they drove away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, oh, my God, [NON-ENGLISH]. She still is alive-- I think she is. I send to-- to him in a package. She lives in a city, you know what the name of the city? Auschwitz-- coincidence. Nice name of a city. Her husband, second husband came from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hear Lucia saying, oh, my God. And Yannick, which is her husband's name, Yannick. Not the second one-- he was also killed by Germans, by the way, a coincidence. Because he belonged to the-- Polish underground. But that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, we have to get rid of her. You want to be killed? I have also a son. I don't want any, nothing to do with it. I'm sorry, she has to leave. She has to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, and she, in the morning, she says, Mary-- oh. In mean time, he wasn't there, Kola. When they started this-- this terrible thing. He went to, I said-- I told him, he went to my brother-in-law to see what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, they weren't in ghetto inside, [INAUDIBLE] they weren't in ghetto. They were not. One of my husband's sister, Smitzel, her husband were not-- they did not have obligation to live in ghetto. Why, you're going to ask? Because he wasn't Jewish, her husband. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a different story, he wasn't Jewish but he converted, whatever. They were out of the ghetto, so far. Then I call, I send him there, Kola, to some-- find out what happened to them-- the old lady, my mother-in-law and they were all there again. And the old lady, my mother-in-law was sitting, maybe she wasn't crying after me, she was crying after her little boy, of my son. I don't know about me, whatever. They weren't very, terribly happy when he married me, because I was very poor, and very broken home, and whatever. Then he said, Mary and the baby is at my place, and she is-- they was terribly happy and glad. And my brother gave him, the non-Jewish brother-in-law, gave him some money. In meantime, when he wasn't there she said, Lucia, Mary, I'm going to give you a peasant old dress, an old skirt, a long skirt. You're going to wear a babushka, you're going to have to go someplace to the village to maybe they will hide you, something they don't know you. I don't know, my husband is driving me crazy. I can't Mary, I'm sorry. I can't I blah, blah, blah. And I said, OK, Lucia, I understand. I will walk out in the streets and I'm dead. It's-- it's 99% dead. And then these little boys are playing, both of them, her son was my son's age. Merrick his name was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, then Kola came in. He said, what's going on here? What happened? What happened? She says, Kola, I don't know. I'm sorry, I have to leave. What do you mean? You can't-- you don't know what's going on outside. And she and Yannick and Lucia and they said, what do you want us to die, also? We are sorry but we can't do anything about it. And he said, listen, did I took her here with this child in order to chase you out now?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we'll leave, we'll leave, we'll leave. He took it. They give him a little money, my brother-in-law. He right away walked out, and we will find a little apartment. Not a little apartment, in a house, one room with a kitchen. Behind the wall, rented the home-- it was a two-family home, behind the wall you can hear everything

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, why don't we stop there. OK.
